coredata.dc:title: Rashba effect on the electronic transport through a quantum dot in the atomic limit
coredata.dc:creator: Santos, Elcivan dos
Frota, H.O.
Ghosh, Angsula
coredata.dc:description: We study the behavior of a quantum dot attached to two electrodes with localized spin–orbit (Rashba) interactions. Using the Anderson model we report the occupation number, specific heat, susceptibility and conductance in the zero-band width limit. We compare our results with those in the absence of the Rashba interactions. The symmetry and regime of various phases demonstrate a strong dependence on the above interactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electronic transport
Quantum-dot
Rashba interaction

head.ce:title: Rashba effect on the electronic transport through a quantum dot in the atomic limit

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Federal University of Amazonas, 69077-000 Manaus, AM, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Federal University of Amazonas, 69077-000 Manaus, AM

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Federal University of Amazonas, 69077-000 Manaus, AM, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We study the behavior of a quantum dot attached to two electrodes with localized spin–orbit (Rashba) interactions. Using the Anderson model we report the occupation number, specific heat, susceptibility and conductance in the zero-band width limit. We compare our results with those in the absence of the Rashba interactions. The symmetry and regime of various phases demonstrate a strong dependence on the above interactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QD), a few nanometers in size, have been at the forefront of research recently due to their unique electronic, optical, magnetic and catalytic properties. They are essential not only in diagnostic imaging, biosensors, drug delivery [1–5] but also in several other technological developments like solar cells, LED, lasers and quantum computing [6,7]. They are generally semiconductors, metals or metal oxides [8]. However, semiconductor quantum dots have drawn a lot of attention due to its distinct “quantum size effects”. Heretofore, chemistry, physics, and materials science have provided methods for the production of quantum dots with tighter control of several associative factors, like, solubility, particle growth, size and emission properties. Several methods are employed for QD generation including heating/combustion [9–11], hydrothermal [12,13], microwave/ultrasonic [14–16], acid oxidation [17–19], laser oblation [20,21] etc.

Spin-related phenomena play an important role especially in problems related to transport properties in low-dimensional systems. A spin-polarized field effect transistor based on the Rashba interaction has motivated various works in narrow gap semiconductors where the gate voltage of the device could be used to modulate the above interaction [22]. Spin–orbit interactions in quantum dots related to electron confinement and symmetry breaking are introduced by the Rashba/Dresselhaus terms. The interaction not only depends on the characteristic of the material but also can be modified by an external electric field. The experimental data [23] on few-electron quantum dots have been studied using Rashba spin–orbit coupling and exchange interaction [24]. The correlation effects in few-electron lateral quantum dot in a fully interacting Hamiltonian of electrons confined in a quantum dot [25,26] have been explored. Rashba dots in a metallic host in the presence of Coulomb interaction was considered to investigate the transport properties [27,28] using the Anderson Hamiltonian. Most of the existing theoretical studies of the spin–orbit coupling in QDs are based on numerical simulations or perturbative methods. Our solution provides an insight to the model based on analytical solutions.

In this work we consider the quasi-zero dimensional quantum dot connected to two-electrodes with Rashba interaction. We investigate in detail the effect of the Rashba interactions of the leads on the electronic, thermodynamic and transport properties of the system. An onsite Coulomb interaction and the hybridization also plays an important role in modifying the characteristics of the system. The paper is organized is as follows. In Section 2, the Anderson impurity model [29] with the Rashba interactions in the zero-band width limit has been discussed. In Section 3 the occupation numbers along with the specific heat, susceptibility and the conductance values are analyzed. Finally a short summary on our results is depicted in Section 4.

The model Hamiltonian is given by

)

ε
η ,

→

α
η
is the Rashba constant,
c
f ν

c
f ν
)
creates (annihilates) an electron with spin ν and energy
ε
f
in the dot orbital, where two electrons interact each other via the Coulomb potential U , and
V
η ,
k
→
is the hybridization energy between the conduction band and the QD. The underlying physics of the model due to hybridization and Rashba interaction can de demonstrated by exact diagonalization in the zero-band width limit, where the conduction states are represented by a single level at the Fermi level of energy. The zero-bandwidth model (ZBWM) has been previously used successfully in various studies [30–34]. ZBWM for the superconducting leads has been successfully used in the Anderson model to yields a phase diagram in good agreement with previous consolidated results [30]. It has been able to explain the magnetic correlations in the two-impurity Anderson model [31]. Despite its simplicity ZBWM has described various experimental results in valence fluctuating model [32] and also a good description of magnetic resonance phenomena in superconductors with kondo impurities [33]. The Josephson effect in a superconductor/triple-quantum-dot ring/superconductor structure has also been elucidated using the ZBWM [34]. In the above limit we can rewrite the Hamiltonian (1) to be,
(5)
H =
∑
j , ν
ε

In order to simplify the process of diagonalization, we consider the symmetry of the Hamiltonian H , taking into account the conserved quantum numbers. The Hamiltonian conserves the charge Q and the parity P which can be defined by the operators
Q =

It is observed that both the operators Q and P commute with H
(
[ H , Q ]
= 0
e
[ H , P ]
= 0
). Therefore, the eigenstates of H can also be chosen to be eigenstates of Q and P and can be labeled by the corresponding quantum numbers
( Q , P )
. Hence, the basis constructed to diagonalize the Hamiltonian H can be classified according to their Q and P values. In this way, there are no matrix elements between states that have different
( Q , P )
and the diagonalization of H can be performed independently in each
( Q , P )
subspace. Thereby, applying the operators Q and P on a eigenstate
| Q , P 〉
we obtain
Q
| Q , P 〉
= Q
| Q , P 〉
and
P
| Q , P 〉
= P
| Q , P 〉
. For example, for the basis state

As the above Hamiltonian commutes with above two operators, it is possible to diagonalize the Hamiltonian in the sub-space containing the charge and the parity. It is important to note that the Hamiltonian (6) can be diagonalized in the (
a
j η
,
f
η
) basis utilizing a 64 × 64 matrix, whereas Q , P requires matrices with much lesser dimensions. The diagonalization of the separate and lesser dimensional matrices: 4 matrices 1 × 1, 8 matrices 3 × 3 and 4 matrices 9 × 9 not only simplifies but also reduces the computational cost considerably. It is important to state that only the 9 × 9 matrices have been diagonalized numerically. With the above considerations we are also able to generate the basis sets of H from the vacuum state with the possible values of ( Q , P ) as given below in Table 1. The above diagonalization and the basis sets thus allow us to calculate the thermodynamic and the transport properties of the above Hamiltonian.

In this section we present a detailed study of the occupancies, the thermodynamic and transport properties of the system. Analyzing the Hamiltonian (6) we observe the presence of the Rashba interaction in the electrodes duplicates the energy levels which can be written
ϵ
i
±
α
i
. However, irrespective of the value of α , the quantum dot has 0,
ϵ
f
or
2
ϵ
f
+ U
values of the energy levels with zero, single or double occupancy respectively. For negative
ϵ
f
and
|
ϵ
f
|
≫
|
ϵ
i
−
α
i
|
and
U = 0
, the occupation tends to be 2 with spin zero, whereas for large U , it approximates to 1 with spin
1 ∕ 2
. In the case of
|
ϵ
f
−
(
ϵ
i
−
α
i
)
|
<
t
1
,
t
2
and large U the occupation number varies between 0 and 1. On the other hand,
|
ϵ
f
+ U |
<
t
1
,
t
2
lead to an occupation number between 1 and 2. Hence, we observe that the occupation number varies between 0 and 2 with a direct impact on the thermodynamic and transport properties of the system. Moreover, the hybridization term is a energy term that leads to the broadening of the levels and its effects can be observed in all the properties studied. The gain in the energy due to the above terms seems to randomize the spin on the magnetic ion leading to more gradual transition between the different levels of occupancies. The specific heat peaks are broadened due to the increase in
t
j
values whereas the magnetic susceptibility decreases with the increase in hybridization. The electrical conductance is highly dependent on the
t
j
values which increases with
t
j
as the increase in
t
j
increases the transition rate between the electrodes via the quantum dot. Moreover, the increase in
t
j
leads to a broadening of the two peaks of
G
( T )
which finally merges to a single one with further increase of
t
j
. It is important to note that all our parameters have been considered in terms of this interaction
t
j
unless mentioned otherwise.

The energy levels for the above Hamiltonian demonstrates a symmetry around
Δ =
ϵ
1
−
ϵ
2
due to the charge conservation and also the electron–hole symmetry. The degeneracy at
Δ = 0
is also notable.

In Fig. 1, the dependencies of the occupation number
n
f
of the ground-state of the quantum dot on the energy
ϵ
f
are presented for various values of U . Fig. 1(a) exhibits the above dependencies in the absence of the Rashba interaction for
ϵ
1
=
ϵ
2
= 0
and
U = 0
(solid line), 10 t (dashed line), 20 t (dotted line) and 40 t (dashed–dotted line).
〈
n
f
〉
varies between 2 for
−
ϵ
f
≫ U
to zero for
ϵ
f
≫ U
with intermediate values in between. The value of
〈
n
f
〉
= 1 . 5
at
ϵ
f
= − U
demonstrates that the fundamental state is a mixture of doubly and singly occupied states. On the other hand
〈
n
f
〉
= 0 . 5
at
ϵ
f
= 0
is a mixture of the vacuum and singly occupied states. Moreover, the width of the plateau decreases with a decrease in U as can be seen from the figure. The ground-state occupation number for
ϵ
1
=
ϵ
2
= 20 t
,
α
1
=
α
2
= 0
and
U = 30 t
is given in Fig. 1(b). The effect of the Rashba interaction
α
1
=
α
2
= 21 t
on
〈
n
f
〉
is exhibited in Fig. 1(c). Unlike Fig. 1(b), the occupation number demonstrates two distinct discontinuities in Fig. 1(c). This can be better understood by remembering that while only two sub-spaces (2,0) and (1,1) forms the fundamental state in Fig. 1(b), three sub-spaces (4, − 1), (3, − 3) and (2, − 1) spans the fundamental state of Fig. 1(c). For a finite temperature, the occupation number has been calculated for various values of the Rashba parameter:
α
1
=
α
2
= α = 0 , 5 t
, 10 t , 15 t , 20 t , 21 t , 22 t , 23 t and 25 t .

The specific heat
C
v
∕
k
B
as a function of temperature (
k
B
T
) is exhibited in Fig. 2 for
ϵ
f
= − 6 t
,
ϵ
1

ϵ
2
= 0
for
U = 12 t
and
α
1
=
α
2
= α
, α
= 0 (solid line), 1 t (dashed line), 2 t (dotted line) and 3 t (dashed–dotted line). The temperature dependence for α
= 0 demonstrates two peaks pertaining to the gaps at low and high energies respectively. An increase in the Rashba parameter increases the gap at low energies and increases the energy levels in the gap-region of high energy leading to broadening of the peak at low energies. The above effect leads to disappearance of the second peak as we increase α .

In Fig. 3 we demonstrate the temperature dependencies of magnetic susceptibility ( χ ) with
ϵ
1
= 6 t
,
ϵ
2
= 2 t
,
ϵ
f
= − 6 t
,
U = 12 t
and
α
1
=
α
2
= α
, α
= 0 (solid line), 1 t (dashed line), 1.5 t (dotted line) and 2 t (dashed–dotted line). For α
= 0, 1 t , 1.5 t ,
k
B
T χ
tends to the value of 0.25 indicating a magnetic state. The ground state defined by (1, − 1) justifies the value of χ . However, for
α = 2 t
, low temperature
k
B
T χ = 0
as the level
ϵ
2 +
=
ϵ
2
− α
contributes to the ground-state along with the quantum-dot level.

Fig. 4 considers the nature of the conductance G in the presence of the Rashba interaction α . The dependencies on
ϵ
f
is given in Fig. 4(a) for
ϵ
1
=
ϵ
2
= 0
,
U = 12 t
,
k
B
T = 1 t
and
α
1
=
α
2
= α
, α
= 0 (solid line), 2 t (dashed line), 4 t (dotted line), 6 t (dashed–dotted line) and 8 t (dashed–double-dotted line). For large and negative
ϵ
f
, G demonstrates a Coulomb-blockade regime, followed by the valence-fluctuating region. It increases initially with
ϵ
f
and reaches its maximum at
ϵ
f
= − U
. On further increase of
ϵ
f
, it transits to the singly occupied configuration and then decreases to its minimum at
ϵ
f
= − U ∕ 2
due to the double occupation of the state. It is important to note that G is proportional (
− d
n
f
∕ d
ϵ
f
) with an excellent agreement regarding the position of the peaks and also its dependence on
ϵ
f
. The effect of Rashba interaction indicates a decrease in the conductivity around
ϵ
f
= − U ∕ 2
and a Coulomb blockade region between the two peaks of G . Fig. 4(b) demonstrates the temperature dependencies of G for
ϵ
1
=
ϵ
2
= 1 t
,
U = 12 t
,
ϵ
f
= − 6 t
and
α
1
=
α
2
= α
, α
= 0 (solid line), 1 t (dashed line), 2 t (dotted line), 3 t (dashed–dotted line), 4 t (dashed–double-dotted line). At low-temperatures, an increase in α decreases G and tends to nullify it at
α = 6 t
where the quantum dot is saturated and prohibits a charge transfer between the electrodes and quantum dot. In Fig. 4(c), G vs.
ϵ
f
plot is exhibited for several temperatures :
k
B
T = 1 t
(solid-line), 1.5 t (dashed-line), 2 t (dotted line) and 4 t (dashed–dotted line) with
ϵ
1
=
ϵ
2
= 0
,
U = 12 t
and
α = 1 t
. The peaks show a decrease in their intensities and also suffers a broadening due to the increase in temperature.

The Rashba effect on the quantum dot placed between the leads has been studied using the Anderson model in the zero-band width limit. The spin–orbit interaction in the leads preserves the electron–hole symmetry and also conserves the charge of the system. The occupation number depends on the Rashba interaction and also is sensitive to
ϵ
f
and U . Moreover, the occupation numbers that depend on α molds the adimensional conductance, specific heat and the magnetic phase of the system. α not only alters the quantitative values but also the qualitative nature of the properties. The disappearance of the second peak in
C
v
, a magnetic − non-magnetic phase transition and the decrease and broadening of the peaks in conductance values demonstrates the importance of α in the model.

Elcivan dos Santos: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Validation. H.O. Frota: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Angsula Ghosh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Brazilian funding agencies CAPES, Brazil - Finance code 001 and CNPq, Brazil .
